---
title: "Inside Airbnb"
author: "Carla du Plessis"
date: "2026-03-05"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

library(tidyverse)
library(leaflet)
library(leaflet.extras)
```

# Read real "listings" dataset of Airbnb's situated in Cape Town 
```{r}
RealListings <- read.csv("C:/Users/duple/OneDrive/Documents/Data Science 874/Post-block Assignments/Airbnb Data/listings.csv")
```

# Visualize and accordingly clean the "price" column (and rows)
```{r}
# ── STEP 1: Parse the raw price string ────────────────────────────────────────
# The price column is stored as a character string (e.g. "$2,315.00")
# We strip the "$" and "," characters and convert to numeric
RealListings <- RealListings %>%
  mutate(price_clean = as.numeric(gsub("[$,]", "", price)))

# ── STEP 2: Inspect before making any decisions ───────────────────────────────
summary(RealListings$price_clean)
sum(is.na(RealListings$price_clean))

# Look at the cheapest and most expensive listings to sense-check extremes
RealListings %>%
  arrange(price_clean) %>%
  select(name, neighbourhood_cleansed, room_type, price_clean) %>%
  head(20)

RealListings %>%
  arrange(desc(price_clean)) %>%
  select(name, neighbourhood_cleansed, room_type, price_clean) %>%
  head(20)

# ── STEP 3: Visualize the raw distribution ────────────────────────────────────
# Log scale is used because price is right-skewed
# This reveals whether extreme values are isolated outliers or part of the tail
ggplot(RealListings %>% filter(!is.na(price_clean)),
       aes(x = price_clean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 100, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(title = "Raw Price Distribution (before cleaning)",
       x = "Price per Night (ZAR, log scale)", y = "Count")

# ── STEP 4: Investigate missing prices before dropping ────────────────────────
# We check whether missing prices are associated with inactive listings
# If so, dropping them loses no real market signal
RealListings %>%
  filter(is.na(price_clean)) %>%
  summarise(
    has_reviews      = sum(number_of_reviews > 0),
    has_availability = sum(has_availability == "t"),
    has_revenue      = sum(estimated_revenue_l365d > 0, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_missing    = n()
  )

# ── STEP 5: Apply cleaning filters  ─────────────────────────
RealListings_clean <- RealListings %>%
  filter(
    # Drop rows where price could not be parsed (scraping gaps or
    # incomplete listings — confirmed above to have little/no activity)
    !is.na(price_clean),
    
    # Drop listings priced at zero — not a realistic Airbnb price,
    # likely placeholder or test listings that were never activated
    price_clean > 0,
    
    # Drop extreme high-end outliers only if inspection (Step 2) showed
    # them to be isolated errors. Adjust this threshold based on what
    # you actually observed — genuine luxury listings should be kept
    price_clean <= 50000
  )

# Report how many rows were retained vs dropped
cat("Original rows:  ", nrow(RealListings), "\n")
cat("Retained rows:  ", nrow(RealListings_clean), "\n")
cat("Dropped rows:   ", nrow(RealListings) - nrow(RealListings_clean), "\n")

# ── STEP 6: Visualize cleaned distribution ────────────────────────────────────
# Compare against Step 3 to confirm cleaning had the intended effect
ggplot(RealListings_clean, aes(x = price_clean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 100, fill = "darkgreen", color = "white") +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(title = "Cleaned Price Distribution",
       x = "Price per Night (ZAR, log scale)", y = "Count")
```
# Check "estimated revenue" column in RealListings_clean dataset
```{r}
# ── Check completeness of estimated_revenue_l365d ────────────────────────────

# Summary overview — shows min, max, NAs and distribution at a glance
summary(RealListings_clean$estimated_revenue_l365d)

# Exact counts broken down by value type
RealListings_clean %>%
  summarise(
    total_rows    = n(),
    na_count      = sum(is.na(estimated_revenue_l365d)),
    zero_count    = sum(estimated_revenue_l365d == 0, na.rm = TRUE),
    valid_count   = sum(estimated_revenue_l365d > 0, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_valid     = round(valid_count / total_rows * 100, 1)
  )

# Valid (> 0) — the listing has recorded booking activity and a meaningful revenue estimate
# Zero — the listing exists but has no recorded bookings in the last year; Inside Airbnb cannot estimate revenue without occupancy data
# NA — the value is entirely missing, likely because the listing was too new or inactive to be estimated at all
```

```{r}

```

```{r}

```

```{r}

```

```{r}

```

```{r}

```

```{r}

```

```{r}

```
